coredata.dc:title: Dual-sized carbon quantum dots enabling outstanding silicon-based photodetectors
coredata.dc:creator: Hsiao, Po-Hsuan
Lai, Yen-Chuan
Chen, Chia-Yun
coredata.dc:description: Broadband and reliable photodetectors were in high demand due to their potential applications for optical communications, sensing and photoelectronic switches. Here, the facile in-situ formation of dual-sized carbon quantum dot (CQD) films featuring dense contact with silicon (Si) substrates that avoided the formation of interface oxide was demonstrated. The unique features of dual-size graded CQDs constituted the bottom layer of 10-nm CQDs functioning as the main light harvester and facilitating the heterojunction establishment with Si, while the top component of 6-nm CQDs at top featuring the electron blocking layer as well as hole extractor. Such heterostructures allowed the low dark current which was an order of magnitude less than monodispersed CQDs, and manifested the sound operation stability under systematic examinations including abrasion test, variations of temperature and environmental pH conditions and long-term switching test. In addition, the dependence of photocurrent on light intensity was fitted with power law relationship, showing the fairly identical characteristics by examining light illuminations of 850 nm, 580 nm, and 352 nm, respectively, which validated the wavelength-independent and compelling photodetection quality. These results represented a significant advancement in photodetector design with all-solution synthesis and pronounced high performances.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Photodetector
Heterostructure
Responsivitity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the dual-sized CQD films
Preparation of the monodispersed CQD films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cross sectional TEM images of (a) monodispersed CQD films and (b) dual-sized CQD films. The inserted figures presented the corresponding cross-sectional SEM images, respectively. Top-view HRTEM images of (c) monodispersed CQD films and (d) dual-sized CQD films. Possible formation mechanism of dual-sized CQD films.
HRTEM investigations of (a)bottom layers of CQDs and (b) upper layers of CQDs in dual-sized CQD films.
(a) XRD patterns, (b) FTIR analysis, (c) Raman results and (d) PL measurements of both monodispersed and dual-sized CQD films.
(a) XPS spcetra and (b) depth profiling of CQD/Si structures. (c) Cross-sectional TEM image of CQD/Si structures. LBIC results of (d) monodispersed CQD films and (e) dual-sized CQD films.
(a) Light-reflection spectra of monodispersed and dual-sized CQD films. Cross-sectional examinations of electric-field distributions of dual-sized CQD films at (d) 352 nm, (e) 580 nm and (d) 850 nm.

(a) Cyclic voltammetry measurements of dual-sized CQD films. (b) Schematic illustration of corresponded band diagram. (c) Transport pathways of photogenerated holes in monodispersed (figure left) and dual-sized (figure right) CQD films, respectively.

(a) Time-dependent dark currents, (b) Dark I-V results, (c) Photovoltaic I-V results and (d) photoresponses under white-light illuminations of monodispersed and dual-sized CQD films, respectively. In addition, the inserted figure in Fig. 7(b) represented the measured ideality diode factor (n) of two CQD-based devices.

Intensity profile of the dual-sized CQD /Si heterostructures along with the corresponding power law fit under light irradiation of different wavelength at (a) 850 nm (b) 580 nm (c) 352 nm, respectively.

Spectral responsivity and detectivity as a function of incident light intensity of the dual-sized CQD /Si heterostructures along with the corresponding power law fit. The different wavelength light source was irradiated at (a) 850 nm (c) 580 nm (e) 352 nm, respectively. Plot of normalized photocurrent to dark current ratio (NDNR) and noise equivalent power (NEP) as a function of different light intensities under (b) 850 nm (d) 580 nm (f) 352 nm irradiations, respectively.

(a) Abrasion test (b) temperature-variation test and (c) environmental pH test of device responsibility. Three various samples including the dual-sized CQD/Si, monodispersed CQD/Si and graphene/Si based devices were compared. (d) 2000 cycles on/off switching test of the dual-sized CQD based photodetectors.
Evaluation of band energies in dual-sized CQDs.
Photovoltaic results and response speed of two types of samples.
Examinations of device performances under illuminations of various wavelengths.

head.ce:title: Dual-sized carbon quantum dots enabling outstanding silicon-based photodetectors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, National Cheng Kung University, Tainan 70101, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, National Cheng Kung University, Tainan 70101, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hierarchical Green-Energy Materials (Hi-GEM) Research Center, National Cheng Kung University, Tainan 70101, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hierarchical Green-Energy Materials (Hi-GEM) Research Center, National Cheng Kung University, Tainan 70101, Taiwan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, National Cheng Kung University, Tainan 70101, Taiwan.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Broadband and reliable photodetectors were in high demand due to their potential applications for optical communications, sensing and photoelectronic switches. Here, the facile in-situ formation of dual-sized carbon quantum dot (CQD) films featuring dense contact with silicon (Si) substrates that avoided the formation of interface oxide was demonstrated. The unique features of dual-size graded CQDs constituted the bottom layer of 10-nm CQDs functioning as the main light harvester and facilitating the heterojunction establishment with Si, while the top component of 6-nm CQDs at top featuring the electron blocking layer as well as hole extractor. Such heterostructures allowed the low dark current which was an order of magnitude less than monodispersed CQDs, and manifested the sound operation stability under systematic examinations including abrasion test, variations of temperature and environmental pH conditions and long-term switching test. In addition, the dependence of photocurrent on light intensity was fitted with power law relationship, showing the fairly identical characteristics by examining light illuminations of 850 nm, 580 nm, and 352 nm, respectively, which validated the wavelength-independent and compelling photodetection quality. These results represented a significant advancement in photodetector design with all-solution synthesis and pronounced high performances.

ce:section.ce:para: Self-powered photodetectors with broadband capability were regard as essential components of advanced optoelectronic devices and extensively correlated with many applications spanning from chemical/biological sensing, communication system, solar cells to active optoelectronics [1–3]. The currently commercial photodetectors are mainly through the bulk silicon (Si) because of their material abundance, remarkable stability, sound chemical resistance and mature Si-based microelectronic technology [4–7]. Nevertheless, the relatively poor carrier mobility and low light absorption have critically impeded the bulk Si for providing broadband photodetection performance [8,9]. In this regard, recent researches indicated that the charge transfer and collection characteristics could be improved through heterostructure design, such as graphene/Si, CuGaTe2/Si, p-NiO NWs/Si, SiQDs/Gr/Si, WS2/Si and many others [10–15]. Nevertheless, there remained a critical challenge related to find the photoactive materials that enabled to harvest the wide range of lights for achieving low-cost, high-speed and broadband photodetection design.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been considered the quasi-spherical graphitic architecture that possessed several superior capabilities such as low cost, nontoxicity, sound chemical stability, and remarkable optical properties [16–19]. Generally, the surfaces CQDs were covered with long-chain functional groups to retain dispersibility in aqueous media [20]. These surface functional groups might effectively inhibit the aggregation between CQDs and further improved their chemical stability [20–22]. Nevertheless, these functional groups essentially formed the insulating shells on CQDs and the involved repulsive charges enlarged the interspacing between CQDs, which could result in the retardation of charge transport in CQD films that led to the inevitable generation of leakage currents [20,23]. It thus turned out to be huge challenge to integrate highly light absorptive CQDs in combination with bulk Si possessing dense and uniform contact for establishing effective junction at interfaces. Also, even the CQD/Si heterojunction could be created, the loosely packed CQD-based films usually caused the inhabitation of their charge transfer characteristics of photogenerated carriers.

In this consideration, the realization of dense CQD films coupled with Si as the broadband photodetectors was expected to provide the great feasibility for achieving both wide operation spectral range and at the same time, boosting the sensing capability including high detectivity, remarkable responsibility and extremely low leakage currents. Moreover, in terms of compatibility with the mature Si technology, among various CQD formation routes solution-processing method for the preparation CQD/Si based devices would be prevailing as the superiorities of allowing facile synthesis, compatible integration and mass production [23]. Taking together, in this study, for the first time we proposed the facile in-situ film preparation of creating the unique dual-sized CQD films directly formed on Si substrates, serving as self-powered photodetectors. The photodetection performances were remarkably improved compared with CQD films containing with sole CQD size, manifesting the ultra-high photoresponsivity of 645.2 mA/W, and detectivity of 3.53x1013 under 580-nm illuminations, which were among the best values of the reported bulk-Si based heterostructure photodetectors.

Glucose (Mw = 180.16, 99% purity) was obtain from Acros Organics. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%) and nitric acid (HNO3, 65%) were purchased form Scharlau and Panreac Application, respectively. The water used in the experiments was purified through a Millipore water purification system (18 MΩ, Milli-Q, Millipore).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the dual-sized CQD films
ce:section.ce:para: 2 M hydrochloric acid with volume of 20 ml and 0.1 ~ 1 g pure glucose as the solute were utilized as precursors. The mixed solution was treated with ultrasonication at 40 kHz for 4 h. After that, the solutions were dropped onto the as-prepared Si substrates and dried at 100 °C for 30 min. Finally, the as-prepared samples were annealed at 400 °C on the hot plate for 1 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the monodispersed CQD films
ce:section.ce:para: 2 ~ 5 g of pure Glucose powder was first calcined in the autoclave at 400 °C for 2 h, and then was carefully ground into fine powders. The as-prepared sample was immersed into the DI water with sonication 40 kHz for 30 min. The dispersed solutions were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane with vacuum pump, and then dried at 70 °C. After that, the as-prepared solution was dropped onto the Si substrates and dried at 100 °C for 30 min.

The interdigitated electrode (80-nm Ag in thickness) with pad size of 200 μm prepared on the CQD/Si samples using electron gun evaporation. The rear electrode was prepared with depositing 100-nm Al through the electron gun evaporation.

The microstructures of CQDs were investigated with transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F). The cross-sectional view of dual-sized CQD films were prepared by means of focused ion beam (FIB, FEI Helios G3CX) technique and followed by conducting lift-off procedure. Morphologies of the CQD/Si heterostructures were characterized with a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S-4800). Chemical-structure analyses of samples were obtained from a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, PerkinElmer Frontier) spectrometer, where each spectrum was recorded from 400 to 4000 cm−1. Crystallographic characterizations were performed with an X-ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS Gmbh) using Cu-K radiation. Luminescent behaviors of the CQD structures were characterized through a photoluminescent (PL) system equipped with a light-emitting diode lamp (output power: 780 mW) and the excited wavelength of the light source was 365 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI 5000 Versa Probe) measurements were carried out utilizing a monochromatic X-ray source (400 μm spot size, 36 W). Spatial current characterizations of devices versus light illumination (laser diode as light source with wavelength of 450 nm and output power of 5 mW) were measured using Laser Beam Induced Current (LBIC) measurement system. Light-reflection spectra were measured with UV–vis-NIR spectrometer (Hitachi U-3900H). Optoelectrical characterizations were performed in a source meter (Keithley 2400), where LED light sources with various wavelengths (192, 254, 352, 420, 580, 633 and 850 nm) were utilized. The transient photoresponse of the devices was investigated by the chronoamperometry method under pulsed monochromatic light by varying the incident light intensity. The cyclic voltammetry measurements were utilized to investigate band structures of dual-sized CQD films.

Fig. 1 (a) presented the regular CQD films combing monodispersed CQDs with average diameter of 10.3 ± 0.63 nm, where the size distribution histogram and the corresponding Gaussian fitting were demonstrated in Supplementary material. By contrast, through the in-situ growth technique, the dual-sized CQD films were created, as evidenced in Fig. 1(b). While the film morphology and thickness remained unchanged, the dual-sized structures essentially contained CQDs with two distinct sizes: the comparably large CQDs with average size of 10.2 ± 0.22 nm as the bottom constituent and few layers of 6.1 ± 0.62-nm CQDs as the upper component were established. To reveal the origin of dual-sized features, HRTEM characterizations were performed to access the interparticle configurations of such two different CQD structures, as demonstrated in Fig. 1(c) and 1(d). In addition to the crystallographic CQDs, the loosely compacted CQDs could be found and in between dots, there exhibited the gel-like features, standing for unreacted precursors, which could be particularly obvious in the case of monodispersed CQD films, as shown in Fig. 1(c). Evidently, the closely packed configuration of dual-sized CQD could be envisioned while no remaining gels could be seen [Fig. 1(d)], which interpreted the complete transformation of entire glucose gels to the crystallographic CQD features.

The possible formation mechanism was schematically illustrated in Fig. 1(e), which could be envisioned by the colloid growth theory [24]. The generation of dual-sized CQDs was attributed to the two-step transformation of glucose precursors into multilayered graphene structures. The first-step in-situ formation of CQDs was readily initiated with the thermal decomposition of glucose and in turn created the nucleus all over the glucose matrix. It should be noted that the critical size of graphene nucleus was directly dependent to the initial concentration of carbon precursors which experienced the competition process between nucleation and ripening reactions. With the high precursor concentration, the nucleation dominated and therefore, numerous graphene dots with size of 10 nm were formed simultaneously, while the effective growth was eventually terminated as long as the CQDs were closely packed. In the second step, the encapsulated gels within the cavities of neighboring CQDs diffused toward the upper region driven by the thermal flow, and thus induced the rapid nucleation where the newly formed nucleus resided on the top of primary 10-nm CQDs. Once created, the subsequently ripening growth was restricted by the reduced heat that was blocked by the primarily formed CQDs in contact with Si substrates. Thus, CQDs with smaller size of around 6 nm were consequently formed on the upper position of dual-sized CQD structures.

To gain more insight into the distinct microstructures in dual-sized CQDs formed through in-situ growth method, HRTEM was employed to characterize the correlated crystal structures, as shown in Fig. 2 (a) and 2(b). It indicated that the CQDs from bottom layer of CQD films (with average sizes of 10 nm) was highly crystalline with monolithic interplanar distance of 0.34 nm, corresponding to the (002) crystallographic plane of graphite structures, as presented in Fig. 2(a) [25]. In addition to the regular hexagonal lattice, it could be observed that the upper 6-nm CQDs of CQD films displayed the zigzag-terminated edges, as evidenced in Fig. 2(b). Such lattice arrangement of 6-nm CQDs was originated from asymmetric stacking of the graphene layers, which profoundly led to the metallic behaviors that facilitated the charge transporting characteristics in dual-sized CQDs [26].

To further access the crystallographic patterns of formed dual-sized CQD films, the corresponding XRD patterns were indexed, where the monodispersed CQD films were also exhibited, as compared in Fig. 3 (a). The central band located at 2θ of 19.2° could be found in both samples, which was attributed to the (002) graphite planes [27]. By estimating the corresponding interlayer spacings, it could be concluded that the average d-spacing in CQDs (0.43 nm) was comparably larger than that of pristine graphite (0.335 nm) located at 2θ of 26.6°, which was indicative of densely packed alkyl groups arising form the octadecyl chains, and no impurities were observed [27]. The diffraction peak was comparably sharp and intense from CQDs made with in-situ growth, revealing the highly crystalline character. These results corresponded well with the findings in HRTEM image, which validated the sound crystallicity of grown CQDs with dual-sized artitectures.

FTIR spectroscopy was used to investigate the bonding composition of CQDs and its functional groups, as indicated in Fig. 3(b). An obvious absorption peak centered at 1641 cm−1 was caused by CC stretching, which was due to the dehydration of glucose to from CC during thermal treatment [28]. The broadband centered at 3392 cm−1 was assigned to be O-H bonding [28,29]. The absorption at 1027 and 1076 cm−1 were attributed to C-O; absorptions at 1360 and 2927 cm−1 also revealed the existence of C-H bonding [29]. A sharp absorption peak at 852 cm−1 was due to CH2 rocking [28]. It should be noted that the signal of C-H bonding in dual-sized CQD samples were comparably weaker. Such findings implied the complete transformation of amorphous gels into crystalline CQDs while the in-situ growth was adopted.

Raman spectra of dual-sized CQD film and the control group, pre-formed CQD films were shown in Fig. 3(c). Two prominent peaks appeared at 1360 and 1570 cm−1, corresponding to the D and G peaks, respectively [30]. The intensity ratio of the D and G band (ID/IG) was a measurement of the disorder extent, and the ratio of sp3/sp2 carbon. In this case, the monodispersed CQD film possessed a strong ID/IG ratio of 0.93, while the dual-sized CQD film showed the comparably lower ratio of 0.36, corresponding to the fact that the defects in dual-sized CQD film, such as oxygenated groups (CO, CO, OCO) in the sp2 carbon site were substantially suppressed [31]. The ID/IG ratio could be further used to correlate the structural properties of the carbon-based samples. The results clarified the decrease of amorphous gels in dual-sized CQD film, and it was in accordance with the previously TEM results for increase of packing density and improvement of film integrity.

In addition, PL spectra of both monodispersed and dual-sized CQD films were measured by monitoring the PL emissions under 365-nm illuminations, as compared in Fig. 3(d). By deconvoluting the PL peaks, two dominant peaks centered at 500 nm and 580 nm could be resolved which corresponded to intrinsic emission and surface state emission of CQDs, respectively, as shown Fig. 3(d). Specifically, the graphene core determined the intrinsic emission of carbon-based nanomaterials, while the functional groups at CQD shells contributed to the surface-state emission [32]. To compare the PL emission ratio of core/surface state in both samples where the distinct values were evaluated to be 0.78 (sole-layer CQDs) and 1.42 (dual-sized CQDs), it could be concluded that the surface-state emission of monodispersed CQDs dominated the PL characteristics, whereas the emission behaviors of dual-sized CQDs were mainly contributed to the core emitting properties. These represented the closely packed CQDs with superior crystallinity were generated in dual-sized structures, and thus facilitated the charge transporting characteristics.

To examine the chemical state of dual-sized CQD films, XPS was carried out for examining the C 1s spectrum, as shown in Fig. 4 (a). The measured spectrum could be further deconvoluted into five different surface components, corresponding to sp2 (CC) at binding energy of 284.1 eV, sp3 (CC, and CH) at 285.5 eV, CO at 286.0 eV, COC at 287.2 eV, as well as CO at 288.5 eV [28]. It should be noted that the XPS data represented the surface components of the CQDs, and thus the relative contents of the core component (CC/CC) of CQDs could be obtained. In addition, the depth profiling of elemental compositions was presented in Fig. 4(b). It indicated that the dominant component of the films within 0 (top of the films) to 20 nm in depth was C element with atomic ratio more than 70%, which corresponded to the CC and/or CC revealed from XPS spectrum. Meanwhile, the O composition gradually decreased and eventually reached approximately zero at CQD/Si interfaces, which interpreted the direct contact of CQD with underlying Si substrates. This could be further supported by the cross-sectional TEM characterizations, manifesting the close attachment of dense CQD layers with Si substrates, which facilitated the establishment of CQD/Si heterojunction enabling the charge separation of photogenerated carriers, as shown in Fig. 4(c). To further explore contact homogeneity of CQDs with Si serving as a heterojunction interface for robustly converting illumination photons to electric currents as a function of spatial positions, LBIC mapping was conducted to compare the photocurrents of both monodispersed and dual-sized CQD structures, as shown in Fig. 4(d) and (e). The comparably high inhomogeneity of induced currents could be detected from sole-size CQD/Si interfaces, where the spatial deviation of current density could be larger than 2.1% while scanning the illumination area of 500 by 500 μm2. In addition, the average currents induced by light irradiation were as low as 1.635 μA. The dramatic improvement of homogeneity in terms of generated currents was achieved with dual-sized CQD/Si heterojunctions, with maximum deviation as low as 0.1%, and the average currents were above 2.011 μA, which was more than 1.2 times beyond that of sole-size CQD/Si. This explicitly indicated the structural homogeneity and stability that bridged the reliable device performances.

Fig. 5 (a) displayed the measured and simulated light-reflectivity spectra of monodispersed and dual-sized CQDs, respectively, which indicated that the good agreement of experimental and calculated results in both cases. Specifically, the relative low light reflectivity was found in dual-sized CQDs with measured (simulated) average reflectivity of 7.21% (6.37%). In addition, the light reflectivity of sole-size CQDs achieved 46.6% (43.8%), where the strong light reflection from surfaces could be also observed by naked eyes, as shown in the inset of Fig. 5(a). These results evidenced the superior light absorption capability appearing in dual-sized CQDs, which essentially created the graded transition of effective refractive index from surrounding air to the Si substrates due to the architecture of two-scale CQDs at nanoscale, and thus effectively suppressed the possible internal reflection of lights. Moreover, these dual-sized CQD not merely served as antireflection layer, but further functioned as broadband light absorptive media that could harvest the wide wavelength range of incoming light. To unveil the optical characteristics of CQD structures under wide wavelengths of light illumination, the normalized field distributions of electric fields on the samples were studied, as presented in Fig. 5(b)–(d). It was identified that the illuminations with different wavelengths accumulated at different parts of the stacked CQDs. Under the 352 nm irradiation, it could be found that the incident lights were particularly harvested at upper layer of 6-nm CQDs (Z = 40–64 nm), mainly originating from the short penetration depth of incoming illuminations. When the wavelength of incidence increases to 580 nm, the vertical stacking of dual-size CQDs could yield the effective accumulation of lights, where the light amplitude was concentrated at Z = 25–64 nm. At longer wavelength (850 nm), the incident lights were strongly trapped at bottom part of dual-sized CQDs (Z = 12–40 nm), leading to the obvious field concentration. Thus, the superposition of absorption bands exhibiting at three various wavelengths created the broadband light-absorption characteristics, which was supportive from the examination of reflection spectra shown in Fig. 5(a).

Combined with the measured bandgap features shown in Supplementary material, cyclic voltammetry measurements were employed to understand the energy level of heterostructures. Fig. 6 (a) demonstrated the involved oxidation potential of dual-sized CQDs by sweeping the applied voltage from −4 to 4 V, where the HOMO level could be expressed as below [23,33],
(1)
HOMO = - E
( Oxidation ) - 4.8
( eV )

The correlated band diagram was demonstrated in Fig. 6(b), and the corresponding results were summarized in Table 1 . On the basis of band bending at interfaces, it facilitated the electron-hole separation: the photogenerated holes became available for charge transfer from upper layers of 6-nm CQDs, across bottom layers of 10-nm CQDs, injected into Si and eventually collected by rear Al electrode. Nevertheless, the high LUMO level (2.93 eV) of 10-nm CQDs layer might allow the transport of photoexcited electrons toward upper Ag electrode, which resulted in the serve charge recombination at electrode surfaces, and eventually degraded the photodetection performances. With the insertion of 6-nm CQDs possessing small LUMO level (2.68 eV) and large bandgap energy (2.4 eV) in between Ag electrode and 10-nm CQDs, it could favor to block the electrons reaching Ag electrode due to built-in potential barrier that minimized the surface recombination. Thus, the established dual-sized CQD structures behaved as combined role including the creation of heterojunction with Si, promotion of light harvesting and selective blocking of electrons. All the implications implied the heterojunction-mediated carrier separation under light illuminations that could facilitate the efficient photodetection performances with broadband capability.

Besides, it should be emphasized that the 6-nm CQDs could also act as hole extractor through facilitating the charge transporting characteristics. As shown in Fig. 6 (b), the assembling of dual-sized CQDs ensured densely packed configurations of two-scale CQDs for allowing the small cross-planar charge transport resistance that favored the collection of photogenerated holes. Nevertheless, it could be found the monodispersed CQDs assembled in loosely packed features, which spatially reduced the effective hole-transport pathways through CQDs and reached upper Ag electrode. Such steric hindrance stemming from the existence of interparticle spacings in adjacent CQD layers was evidently valid when employing the EIS measurements, showing that the charge transport resistances were 409.1 Ω in dual-sized CQDs and 528.6 Ω in monodispersed CQDs, as demonstrated in Supplementary material. Thus, the designed photodetection materials combined graded CQDs for light harvesting, 10-nm CQD/Si for heterojunction creation and 6-nm CQDs for electron blocking/hole extractor.

The I-V curves of employed CQD-based devices under dark condition were measured, as demonstrated in Fig. 7 (a). The measured dark currents from monodispersed CQD photodetectors reached 3.92 × 10−8 A at initial stage, and gradually saturated to 3.12 × 10−8 A while the applied voltage lasted 60 s, as clearly evidenced in the inset of Fig. 7(a). This could be attributed to the fact that the synthesized monodispersed CQDs possessed the abundant gels filled in the interparticle regions that caused the tendency toward absorb the water or oxygen from surrounding environment, and thus the dark currents were large initially and then reduced to the low saturated value through the desorption of water and oxygen driven by the employment of bias. These features implied the serve leakage behavior that might degrade photodetection reliability. By contrast, the comparably low dark currents were found in dual-sized CQD based detectors, whose saturated dark currents were 3.25 × 10−9 A and maintained the stable trends when the operation lasted for more than 120 s. The corresponding low dark current showed an order of magnitude less than monodispersed CQDs, visualizing the rational design of optoelectronic devices with more reliable detection performances for the practical requirement.

Fig. 7(b) demonstrated the dark I-V results of two samples. The forward-to-reverse current ratio was approximately 596.3 for monodispersed CQDs and 18312.5 for dual-sized CQDs, which indicated the fact that dual-sized CQDs in conjunction with Si substrates achieve sound rectifying behavior. Accordingly, the ideality diode factor was evaluated as follows [34],
(2)
n =
q
kT
dV
dlnI
where q represented the electron charge, K the Boltzmann constant, V the applied voltage and I the excited currents. The corresponding values were 1.68 for monodispersed CQDs and 1.05 for dual-sized CQDs, where the ideality diode factor of 1.05 closely approaching to 1 represented the ideal diode design that readily allowed the efficient rectification of current flow. In addition to the examination of dark currents, investigations of photocurrents under the direct sunlight illuminations were demonstrated, as shown in Fig. 7(c). The correlated device performances and involved built-in potentials based on two types of CQDs were also summarized in Table 2 and Table S3 of the Supplementary Material, respectively. Under AM 1.5 G illuminations, the corresponding photovoltaic parameters of monodispersed (dual-sized) CQDs were 0.46 V (0.59 V) of open-circuit voltage, 9.2 mA (12.2 mA) of short-circuit current density and 53% (61%) of fill factor, respectively. In addition, the series resistance of monodispersed CQDs (11.0 Ω) was found to be much greater than that of dual-sized CQDs (4.3 Ω), again visualizing that the densely packed dual-sized CQDs possessed the superior charge-transport capability. The findings were also supported with the measurement of EIS results, as evidenced in Supplementary material.

The correlated photoresponses under white-light illuminations were demonstrated in Fig. 7(d). In addition, the CQD-based devices were illuminated with switching light of different wavelengths, including 850 nm, 633 nm, 580 nm, 420 nm, 352 nm and 254 nm, and their repetitive on/off cycles were also recorded, as shown in Supplementary material. Fig. 7(d) showed that the dual-sized CQDs possessed the higher photoresponse (0.208 mA), which was beyond 5 times than that of monodisperse CQDs (0.038 mA), In addition, the corresponding rising time (decaying time) was found to be 26 μs (56 μs) in dual-sized CQDs, and 53 μs (40 μs) in monodispersed CQDs, where rising time was defined as the time interval for the response to rise from 10% to 90% of the on/off switch peak value, and decaying time was estimated as the time interval for the response to decrease from 90% to 10% of peak value [23]. The smaller rising time of dual-sized CQDs with broad spectral range under zero bias condition indicated the efficient charge generation, and allowed the separation of electron/hole pairs due to the established CQD/Si heterojunction. On the other hand, the larger decaying time was mainly determined by low probability of recombination processes in the heterostructures [35]. The corresponding results of carrier lifetime from both monodispersed and dual-sized CQDs were presented in Supplementary material.

Relationship between photocurrent and the involved power intensities of the incident light was quantitatively analyzed, where the device photocurrents were measured under zero bias, as shown in Fig. 8 . The photocurrent measurements were carried out from low to high power intensities of incident lights to diminish the possible artifacts caused by the persistence or temperature transients. The correlation of photocurrents versus power intensity in dual-sized CQD/Si heterostructures under various light irradiations at 850 nm, 580 nm, and 352 nm were fitted with power law (
I
ph
∝
P
θ
), respectively, where Iph was the excited photocurrents, P the power density of the incident light, and θ the parameter related to the trapping and recombination processes of the carrier in optoelectronic devices [36,37]. In this consideration, the fittings displayed θ = 0.73, 0.80 and 0.71 with the corresponding light sources of 850 nm, 580 nm, 352 nm, respectively. The fairly identical results of θ independent to the illimitation wavelengths manifested the characteristics reliably broadband photoresponse. Also, the θ values from various illuminations all fell in the criteria of 0.5 < θ < 1, which were identified to behave closely to the nearly ideal photodetector, verifying the high photodetection quality of the dual-sized CQD/Si heterostructures.

The device feasibility was further examined with evaluating spectral responsivity (R), detectivity (D*) and response speed, respectively [38]. To explore the broadband photodetection performance of the dual-sized CQD/Si heterostructures, the R and D* were examined under 850 nm, 580 nm and 352 nm illuminations, respectively, as shown in Fig. 9 . Basically, R was defined as follows,
(3)
R =
I
ph
-
I d
P
where Iph and Id were photocurrent and dark current, respectively, and P represented the incident power. Moreover, D* was expressed as below,
(4)
D
∗
= R
S
2 q I
d
were S represented the effective area of device, and q was the absolute value of the electron charge. The maximum values of recorded R under 850-nm, 580-nm and 352-nm illuminations were 389.1 mA/W, 645.2 mA/W, and 284.6 mA/W, respectively. Surprisingly, the case of visible illuminations (580 nm) resulted in the comparably outstanding responsivity, which was beyond 1.2 times higher than the commercial Si based photodetectors, as presented in Table 3 . Likewise, the maximum value of D* under 850-nm, 580-nm and 352-nm illuminations with the incident light power of 21 μW/cm2 were 2.12 × 1013 jones, 3.53 × 1013 jones and 1.55 × 1013 jones, respectively. It should be noted that the values of R and D* were investigated at zero-bias condition. In the case of 352 nm illumination, the dual-sized CQD/Si heterostructures showed both comparably low R and D*, which could be attributed to the photon energy of short-wavelength (352 nm) was fairly large to excite electrons to the surface states of CQDs that represented the possible recombination center of photoexcited carriers [39]. In addition, the normalized photocurrent to dark current ratio (NPDR = R/Id) and the noise equivalent power (NEP = 1 / NPDR(2q/Id)1/2), as the important performance indicator of a photodetector was examined [40]. The corresponding results of NPDR and NEP with respect to the light intensities were presented in Fig. 9. In the case of 580 nm, the estimated NPDR and NEP was 1.72 × 107 /W and 2.83 × 10−14 W/Hz1/2, respectively. Similarly, under 850 nm and 352 nm illuminations the NPDR (NEP) values were 1.03 × 107 /W (4.7 × 10−14 W/Hz1/2) and 7.55 × 106 /W (6.44 × 10−14 W/Hz1/2), respectively. The considerably high NPDR and low NEP validated the fact that the dual-sized CQD-based devices could function well under the ultralow illuminations of wideband wavelengths. In addition, the performance comparisons of dual-sized CQD/Si photodetectors with other designs in literature were summarized in Supplementary material.

In spite of providing sound device performance, the operation stability of a vast number of advanced photodetector designs still remained questionable. Thus, systematic reliability tests were performed for examining three photoactive designs, including the dual-sized CQD/Si, monodispersed CQD/Si and graphene/Si based devices. Fig. 10 (a) presented the abrasion test under the various weight loadings from 0 to 200 g to validate the wearing capability of devices. It should be noted that the device responsibility of graphene/Si based devices was comparably weak due to the fact that the light absorption of graphene in UV and visible regions was low. In addition, the results indicated that the device responsibility of dual-sized CQD/Si samples maintained the sound reliability, which showed only 4.7% of response reduction with 200 g of loadings. By contrast, under the same loading weight, the reduction of responsibility reached 48.6% and 54.1% for monodispersed CQD/Si and graphene/Si based devices, respectively.

In line with abrasion test, three various samples were subjected to thermal treatment under the variations of temperature, as shown in Fig. 10(b). It could be observed that even the temperature was risen to 115 °C, the dual-sized CQD/Si based devices exhibited the trivial reduction of responsibility by 1% due to the densely packed features and less defects at CQD/Si interfaces; the significant reductions of device responsibility by 13.1% and 13.5% at 115 °C were found in monodispersed CQD/Si and graphene/Si cases, respectively. In addition, the dependence of treating three various samples with wide range of pH values (2–12) to device responsibility was explored, as presented in Fig. 10(c). The almost unchanged features regarding the variation of pH values was found from dual-sized CQD/Si samples, with the reduction of responsibility by 0.24% at both pH = 2 and 11, respectively. Nevertheless, at pH = 2 the obvious structural damages were created from monodispersed CQDs, which could be attributed to the decomposition of unreacted gels in acidic environment that caused the vital decrease of corresponding responsibility by 41.2%. Finally, the on–off transient switching response of dual-sized CQD/Si photodetectors was tested for 2000 cycles, as presented in Fig. 10(d). Under such long-term test, no obvious reduction of photoreponse could be found, verifying the stable and reliable operation capability of device performance that could be potential for practical use.

Broadband photodetectors were prepared with facile in-situ growth that directly employed the dual-sized CQD layers on Si substrates functioning as the heterostructure architectures. High performance photodetection characteristics were contributed from the combination of dual-size graded CQDs including bottom layer of 10-nm CQDs as the main light harvester and responding to the establishment CQD/Si heterojunction, and 6-nm CQDs at top featuring the electron blocking layer as well as hole extractor. The elaborately designed heterostructures allowed the low dark current which was an order of magnitude less than monodispersed CQDs. Moreover, under the systematic examinations including abrasion test, variations of temperature and environmental pH conditions, and long-term switching test, the results visualized the rational design of optoelectronic devices with more reliable detection performances. More importantly, the recorded responsivitity under 580-nm illuminations was 645.2 mA/W, which was more than 1.2 times higher than the commercial Si based photodetectors. The design strategy might benefit the practical employment of high-performance, low-cost and reliable optoelectronic design and other functional applications.

Po-Hsuan Hsiao: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Data curation, Validation, Formal analysis. Yen-Chuan Lai: Investigation, Formal analysis, Visualization. Chia-Yun Chen: Conceptualization, Validation, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Data curation, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan (MOST 107-2221-E-006-013-MY3), and Hierarchical Green-Energy Materials (Hi-GEM) Research Center, from The Featured Areas Research Center Program within the framework of the Higher Education Sprout Project by the Ministry of Education (MOE) and the Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST 107-3017-F-006 -003) in Taiwan. The authors greatly thank the Instrument Center and Center for Micro/Nano Science and Technology, National Cheng Kung University with the facilities provided for conducting material characterizations.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.148705.

ce:section.ce:para:
